title: Quiz: Data Compression
description: Test your understanding of source coding theory and compression algorithms

questions.question: According to Shannon's source coding theorem, what is the minimum average number of bits per symbol needed to encode a source with entropy H(X) = 2.5 bits?
questions.explanation: Shannon's source coding theorem states that entropy H(X) is the fundamental lower bound. We cannot compress below 2.5 bits per symbol on average while maintaining lossless compression.

questions.question: For a prefix-free code with codeword lengths {1, 2, 3, 3}, what is the value of the Kraft sum?
questions.explanation: Kraft sum = 2^(-1) + 2^(-2) + 2^(-3) + 2^(-3) = 0.5 + 0.25 + 0.125 + 0.125 = 1.0. This is exactly at the Kraft inequality boundary, indicating an optimal code.

questions.question: Huffman coding is optimal among which class of codes?
questions.explanation: Huffman coding produces an optimal prefix-free (instantaneous) code, minimizing average length among all prefix-free codes for a given distribution. Arithmetic coding can sometimes do better.

questions.question: Arithmetic coding can achieve an average rate exactly equal to the entropy H(X) for any sequence length.
questions.explanation: False. Arithmetic coding approaches entropy as the message length increases, but for finite messages, there's always a small overhead to specify the interval. It achieves H(X) + O(1/n) bits per symbol.

questions.question: According to the AEP, for n i.i.d. symbols from a source with entropy H, approximately how many typical sequences are there?
questions.explanation: The AEP shows that most probability mass concentrates on about 2^(nH) typical sequences, each with probability approximately 2^(-nH). This is why we need nH bits to identify them.

questions.question: A source has 4 symbols with probabilities {0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125}. What is the optimal Huffman code average length?
questions.explanation: Optimal code: A=0, B=10, C=110, D=111. Average length = 0.5(1) + 0.25(2) + 0.125(3) + 0.125(3) = 0.5 + 0.5 + 0.375 + 0.375 = 1.75 bits. This equals entropy for this distribution.

questions.question: A sequence is typical if its empirical probability is close to its probability under the source distribution.
questions.explanation: False. A sequence is typical if -log(p(sequence))/n is close to H(X), not if p(sequence) is close to any specific value. Typical sequences have probability close to 2^(-nH), but this value decreases exponentially with n.

questions.question: Which statements about compression are correct? (Select all)

options.text: Lossless compression below entropy is impossible
Huffman codes have average length ≥ H(X)
Arithmetic coding requires knowing all symbol probabilities exactly
Universal compressors can achieve entropy rate without knowing the source

questions.explanation: a) Correct: Shannon's theorem. b) Correct: No prefix-free code can beat entropy. c) Incorrect: Adaptive arithmetic coding estimates probabilities on the fly. d) Correct: LZ and similar algorithms are universal compressors.
